SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims (coattention + iterative decoder improve SQuAD performance) are supported by clear model definitions (Eq.1–4 for coattention; Eq.5–12 and HMN for the decoder) and empirical results including leaderboard numbers and ablations (Section 4.2 and Table 2 show a 1.9 F1 drop when replacing coattention and gains from iterative HMN). The mathematical formulations are straightforward and appear correct, and the experimental setup uses standard baselines and sensible ablations (e.g. no-attention baseline in Appendix A.1). Minor issues: there are missing details about ensemble construction and sentinel ablation, and no statistical significance testing is reported, but these do not materially undermine the presented conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper combines known components (coattention, maxout/highway layers, pointer-style decoding) but introduces a distinct coattentive encoder that fuses question and document contexts (Eq.1–4, sentinel vector) and—crucially—a dynamic pointing decoder that iteratively updates start/end span estimates via an LSTM + HMN to escape local maxima (Sec.2.2–2.3). This iterative span-refinement is a non-trivial extension beyond single-pass pointer/Match-LSTM approaches (Wang & Jiang) and differs from prior co-attention work (Lu et al.) by its text-focused fusion and decoding loop, so the contribution is notably novel though building on existing techniques.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized (Intro → model → experiments → conclusion) and states the main contributions early (Intro describes the Dynamic Coattention Network and reports concrete SQuAD results: 75.9% F1 single, 80.4% ensemble). Notation is largely consistent and defined (e.g., D, Q, affinity L, AQ/AD, U), though the HMN section is dense and some tensor-shape/parameter descriptions could be clearer for readers unfamiliar with maxout pooling. Figures/tables are referenced throughout (Fig. 1–7, Table 1–2) and the appendices provide informative examples, but the provided text contains repeated paragraphs and a few minor typos/formatting slips (e.g., “steming”, “Commision”) that reduce polish.
